findings.quote: Use this skill when writing logging, telemetry, trace, or diagnostic output around redactable types.
findings.suggestion: The first body line nearly duplicates the frontmatter description. Remove it or replace with a brief sentence that adds context beyond the description.

findings.quote: Prefer boundaries that require redactable marker traits or redacted output wrappers.
findings.suggestion: The word 'Prefer' implies a should-do heuristic but does not state when it is acceptable to deviate. Add a brief note on when a non-marker boundary is acceptable.

findings.quote:
Bad patterns:

- direct `println!`, `eprintln!`, or `dbg!`
- direct `log::info!` or `tracing::info!` with raw values
findings.suggestion: The bad-patterns list names anti-patterns but does not show a before/after code snippet demonstrating the fix. Adding one concrete bad-to-good code pair would improve transfer.

findings.quote:
- `format!("{value}")` or `value.to_string()` on a sensitive value
- serializing a sensitive value and assuming serde will redact it
findings.suggestion: These anti-patterns would benefit from a one-line rationale explaining why serde serialization does not guarantee redaction and what happens at runtime when it is used incorrectly.

envelope.summary: Strong point: the description is exemplary — it names the action (writing logging/telemetry output), trigger (redactable types at sink boundaries), and outcome (enforced redaction) in a single concise sentence. Weak point: anti-patterns are listed but lack inline rationale and before/after code, which limits their teaching value (anti-patterns/missing-rationale). Highest-leverage next action: add a single before/after code snippet to the bad-patterns section showing a raw tracing call replaced by the redacted equivalent, addressing both examples-and-templates/missing-counter-example and anti-patterns/missing-rationale in one edit.

findings.quote:
Use this skill when writing logging, telemetry, trace, or diagnostic output around redactable types.

Redaction must happen before data reaches the sink.
findings.suggestion: Merge the repeated trigger sentence with the sink definition so the body opens with the non-obvious boundary rule.

findings.quote:
It returns `RedactedOutput`, which is either:

- `Text(String)`
- `Json(serde_json::Value)` when the `json` feature is enabled

Accepting `ToRedactedOutput` is safer than accepting `Display`, `Debug`, or `Serialize`, because it says the caller must provide a redacted representation or an explicit non-sensitive wrapper.
findings.suggestion: Keep the explanatory content, but introduce it with a direct agent action such as `Accept ToRedactedOutput at custom sink boundaries`.

findings.quote: Use this boundary template for custom sinks. Keep the wrapper requirement at the public boundary and write only the converted `RedactedOutput` to the sink.
findings.suggestion: This is a strong reusable template; keep it as the central transfer example.

findings.quote:
Bad patterns:

- direct `println!`, `eprintln!`, or `dbg!`
- direct `log::info!` or `tracing::info!` with raw values
- `format("{value}")` or `value.to_string()` on a sensitive value
- serializing a sensitive value and assuming serde will redact it
findings.suggestion: The anti-pattern list is concrete and tied to the safe-boundary guidance; preserve this shape in related skills.

envelope.summary: Strong point: the skill gives concise discovery metadata, concrete safe-boundary examples, a reusable custom-sink template, and explicit anti-patterns. Weak point: the opening repeats the trigger and some sections shift into explanatory prose rather than direct instructions, as noted in v1.conciseness.minor-repetition and v1.imperative-voice.explanatory-prose. Highest-leverage next action: tighten the first two body paragraphs and convert the few explanatory passages into imperative guidance while preserving the template and anti-pattern structure.

findings.quote: Use when writing logging, telemetry, trace, or diagnostic output around redactable types to enforce redaction before data reaches sinks.
findings.suggestion: Description names the trigger (writing logging/telemetry output), action (enforce redaction), and outcome (before data reaches sinks) concisely. No change needed.

findings.quote: Do not pass raw values directly to tracing fields unless the project has its own wrapper that enforces redactable safety.
findings.suggestion: This repeats guidance already established in the Safe Boundaries section ('Replace raw sink calls with wrapper-based calls before values reach the sink'). Consider removing the duplicated prohibition from the Tracing section.

findings.quote: Accept `ToRedactedOutput` at custom sink boundaries when callers must pass an explicit redacted or non-sensitive wrapper.
findings.suggestion: The Custom Pipelines section reads as mandatory but the boundary between must-use and recommended patterns is implicit. Consider labelling the template as a MUST for new custom sinks vs. a SHOULD for existing pipelines.

findings.quote: Treat every logging path as a sink boundary.
findings.suggestion: Strong opening imperative sets the tone for the entire skill. Maintained consistently throughout.

findings.quote: A sink is anything that leaves normal program memory: logs, traces, error reports, metrics labels, support dumps, CLI output, or debug files.
findings.suggestion: Key term 'sink' is defined inline on first use and used consistently throughout. Good practice.
Cross-references properly defer related context to companion skills. Core instructions come first, integrations follow, edge cases last.
The custom sink boundary template is reusable and well-structured. Multiple examples cover slog, tracing, and custom pipelines with both good and bad patterns.

findings.quote:
Enable the `tracing` feature for tracing marker support.

Use `.tracing_redacted()` for display-string output.
findings.suggestion: The Tracing section has one prohibition but no concrete bad-code example like the Safe Boundaries section does. Add a bad/good tracing snippet pair similar to lines 36-41 to make the anti-pattern first-class here.

envelope.summary: Strong point: the skill excels at providing concrete, reusable code templates with paired bad/good examples across multiple integration targets (slog, tracing, custom pipelines). Weak point: some guidance is repeated across sections (e.g., the 'do not pass raw values' prohibition appears in both Safe Boundaries and Tracing), and the Tracing section lacks a concrete bad-code example to match other sections. Highest-leverage next action: add a bad/good code pair to the Tracing section (finding anti-patterns/missing-section-coverage) to bring anti-pattern coverage to the same standard as Safe Boundaries and Custom Pipelines.

findings.quote: description: "Use when writing logging, telemetry, trace, or diagnostic output around redactable types to enforce redaction before data reaches sinks."
findings.suggestion: Preserve this concise trigger, action, and outcome structure because it gives the agent a clear discovery signal.

findings.quote: Types deriving `Sensitive`, `SensitiveDisplay`, `NotSensitive`, and `NotSensitiveDisplay` implement `SlogRedacted` when the feature is enabled.
findings.suggestion: Convert isolated reference prose into command-shaped guidance where practical, for example by starting with the action the agent should take.

envelope.summary: Strong point: the skill gives crisp boundary rules, concrete safe and unsafe patterns, and reusable Rust templates. Weak point: a few reference sentences are descriptive rather than imperative, as noted in imperative-voice.reference-prose-lapses. Highest-leverage next action: make those isolated reference notes command-shaped while preserving the existing examples and anti-patterns.
